title: JustLogic: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Evaluating Deductive Reasoning in Large Language Models
authors: Michael K. Chen (Raffles Institution, Singapore); Xikun Zhang (College of Computing & Data Science, Nanyang Technological University, Singapore); Dacheng Tao (College of Computing & Data Science, Nanyang Technological University, Singapore)
venue: arXiv preprint (cs.CL primary; cs.AI, cs.LG, cs.LO), v1 2025-01-24 / v2 2025-05-09; LaTeX source uses the icml2025.sty style with the [accepted] option, strongly implying ICML 2025 acceptance, but this is NOT independently confirmed by arXiv abs-page metadata (no journal-ref present). Code/data: https://github.com/michaelchen-lab/JustLogic
claim: Existing deductive-reasoning benchmarks are inadequate because they lack task complexity (either natural-language or argument complexity, rarely both), let prior world knowledge substitute for actual reasoning, and offer only superficial error analysis. JustLogic is a programmatically generated benchmark — propositional-logic argument structures rendered into natural language via GenericsKB sentences — that is simultaneously linguistically complex, prior-knowledge-independent (real-world sentences but randomized truth values), and depth/argument-form annotated for fine-grained error analysis. On JustLogic, most SOTA LLMs underperform the human average (73.0%) and all models fall well short of the human ceiling (100.0%), with only DeepSeek R1 (80.9%) substantially exceeding the human average.
novelty: The authors claim three simultaneous contributions no prior benchmark combines (Table 1, Sec 1): (i) a code-generation pipeline that inserts real GenericsKB-Best sentences into synthetically-derived argument structures, giving synthetic datasets' argument complexity (infinite depth, 7 formal argument forms) together with human-curated datasets' natural-language complexity (Flesch-Kincaid 20.55, 10557 vocabulary across >10 domains, on par with FOLIO/LogiQA 2.0 per Table 3); (ii) a novel 'prior knowledge independence test' (QO->A, no context) that empirically measures whether a benchmark leaks answerability via world knowledge, applied to JustLogic and 4 baselines; and (iii) programmatic exposure of reasoning depth (1-7) and argument form as annotated instance metadata, enabling fine-grained error analysis (Sec 5.3, Appendix H) not available for manually curated benchmarks. They also frame JustLogic as 'future-proof' against benchmark leakage/saturation because new test sets of the same difficulty (or higher depths, 8-11, Appendix H.3) can be regenerated trivially.

protocol.description: Standard CQO -> A single-turn classification per instance: a context paragraph of premises C, a fixed question Q ('Is the statement S true, false, or uncertain?'), and 3 answer options O = {true, false, uncertain}; the model must output one label A in one forward pass. Zero-shot, few-shot (3-shot, chosen over 6/9-shot because it produced highest accuracy per Appendix E), and CoT (3 worked examples) prompting variants were tested; additionally self-consistency-CoT (5 sampled paths, majority vote) and tree-of-thought (candidate-generation + candidate-evaluation prompts, max steps = depth+2) were tested in Appendix H.1, with Table 10 reporting values for all four non-reasoning models (Llama3-8B, Llama3-70B, GPT-4o-mini, GPT-4o), though the H.1 prose describes selecting only 'the best small and large non-reasoning models, Llama3-8B-Instruct and GPT-4o' -- a discrepancy present in the source paper itself between its prose (2 models) and its own Table 10 (4 populated model rows). Reasoning models (o1, o1-mini, DeepSeek R1, R1-Distill-Qwen-14B) were evaluated only with CoT since they 'do not require specific prompting techniques due to their reasoning-specific training' (Sec 5.2/Appendix H.1).
protocol.observation_content: Each call receives the full problem in one shot: a preamble with (i) task requirements, (ii) the list of 7 argument-form names (Modus Ponens, Modus Tollens, Hypothetical Syllogism, Disjunctive Syllogism, Reductio ad absurdum, Constructive Dilemma, Disjunction Elimination), and (iii) the 3 answer options, followed by the paragraph of premises and the query statement. Verbatim general-instruction prompt (Appendix E): 'You are given a paragraph of facts/premises, followed by a statement. Perform logical reasoning with propositional logic on the paragraph to determine the truth value of the statement. Here is the list of argument forms: [7 forms listed]. You must answer with either one of the 3 options: TRUE: When the premises in the paragraph lead to the statement / FALSE: When the premises in the paragraph directly contradict the statement / UNCERTAIN: When the premises in the paragraph neither support nor contradict the statement. Do not use your prior knowledge; your answer must be solely determined by the information within the paragraph. Assume that all premises in the paragraph are true. Question: Is the statement true, false, or uncertain?' Few-shot/CoT variants prepend 3 worked demo examples to this same preamble. Tree-of-thought is the one exception to pure single-shot: it runs an explicit multi-step loop (candidate generation prompt + candidate evaluation prompt at each step, up to depth+2 steps) where the model sees accumulated partial reasoning across turns before finalizing TRUE/FALSE/UNCERTAIN (Appendix E, H.1).

representations.variants_compared: Symbolic/formal notation (p, q, r; ->, v, not) shown only in the paper's illustrative Figure 1 -- NOT provided to models ('Formal notations are included for illustrative purposes and are not provided to models', Fig 1 caption). Models only ever see natural-language renderings drawn from GenericsKB-Best sentence templates (Table 2) with 7 explicit named argument forms in the prompt preamble.
representations.prompt_variants_and_effect: 0-shot vs few-shot (3-shot) vs CoT (3-shot with reasoning): CoT was generally best for non-reasoning models (Table 6) -- e.g. Llama3-8B rose from 49.8 (0-shot) to 57.8 (CoT); Llama3-70B rose from 53.1 to 64.6; GPT-4o rose from 53.8 to 65.6. Exception: GPT-4o-mini's CoT (51.8) was slightly worse than its few-shot (54.7). Appendix H.1/Table 10 additionally shows self-consistency-CoT and tree-of-thought (ToT) rarely beat vanilla CoT except for GPT-4o (ToT 71.4% > CoT 65.6%); ToT often hurt smaller models (Llama3-8B ToT 38.6% vs CoT 57.8%) because 'Tree-of-thought is too complex for most models to utilize, often hallucinating across prompts and failing to break down the problem into coherent steps.' A CoT variant that omits explicit propositional-logic terminology ('CoT w/o prop. logic') performed worse than standard CoT for 3 of 4 models tested, e.g. GPT-4o 67.4 vs 65.6 is an exception but Llama3-8B/70B and GPT-4o-mini all dropped slightly, leading the authors to conclude 'the explicit mention of propositional logic in the prompt is generally helpful towards model performance' (Appendix H.1).

models.name: Llama3-8B-Instruct
models.key_results: 0-shot 49.8%, few-shot 41.8%, CoT 57.8% (best), SC-CoT 54.6%, ToT 38.6%, CoT-w/o-logic 54.0% (Table 6, Table 10).

models.name: Llama3-70B-Instruct
models.key_results: 0-shot 53.1%, few-shot 57.8%, CoT 64.6% (best), SC-CoT 58.6%, ToT 60.6%, CoT-w/o-logic 58.3% (Table 6, Table 10).

models.name: GPT-4o-mini-2024-07-18
models.key_results: 0-shot 53.0%, few-shot 54.7% (best), CoT 51.8%, SC-CoT 50.3%, ToT 48.6%, CoT-w/o-logic 50.0% (Table 6, Table 10).

models.name: GPT-4o-2024-05-13
models.key_results: 0-shot 53.8%, few-shot 58.3%, CoT 65.6%, SC-CoT 67.1%, ToT 71.4% (best of all variants for this model), CoT-w/o-logic 67.4% (Table 6, Table 10). Second/third-best non-reasoning model overall on CoT.

models.name: OpenAI o1-mini-2024-07-18
models.key_results: CoT 62.0% (Table 6). Comparable to DeepSeek R1 at low depths but sharp decline at medium/high depths, no longer beating non-reasoning models GPT-4o/Llama3-8B at those depths (Sec 5.3).

models.name: OpenAI o1-2024-12-17
models.key_results: CoT 72.9% (Table 6), 2nd-best overall model; evaluated on only 70 random samples due to limited access (Sec 4.2). Substantially underperforms DeepSeek R1 (80.9%) despite comparable performance on other benchmarks -- traced (Sec 5.2, Appendix H.2, Fig 8) to o1 answering 'Uncertain' for 90% of depth=7 questions without faithfully engaging, and shorter responses at depth>=5 than depth 3-4, suggesting test-time compute was 'artificially limited.'

models.name: DeepSeek R1
models.key_results: CoT 80.9% (Table 6) -- best-performing model overall, by a large margin; only model to exceed human average (73.0%) but still below human ceiling (100.0%). Maintains only a 'moderate decline' at higher depths vs o1-mini's 'sharp decline' (Sec 5.3, Fig 8). Under futureproofing extension (Appendix H.3, Table 11) at depths 8-11: Easy 90.0%, Medium 83.3%, Hard 68.0%, Very Hard 65.0%.

headline_numbers: Best model DeepSeek R1: 80.9% accuracy (CoT), Table 6 / Sec 5.2 -- the only model to exceed the human average.
Human average 73.0% vs human ceiling 100.0% (Table 6, Sec 5.2) -- most SOTA models fall below the human average; all models fall well short of the human ceiling.

Prior-knowledge-independence test |Delta| (Table 5, Sec 5.1): JustLogic 0.4 (accuracy 33.7% vs random 33.3%) vs CLUTRR 2.0, ProofWriter 3.7, FOLIO 6.7, LogiQA 2.0 27.1 -- JustLogic is the most prior-knowledge-independent of the 5 benchmarks tested (replicated with Llama3-70B in Table 9: JustLogic |Delta| 6.4, still lower than FOLIO 10.0 and LogiQA 2.0 23.3).

Diminishing returns from model scale vs prompting: zero-shot Llama3-8B 49.8% vs zero-shot Llama3-70B 53.1% (Table 6); CoT Llama3-8B (57.8%) exceeds zero-shot Llama3-70B (53.1%) despite being a much smaller model (Sec 5.2) -- 'improvements offered by increasing model size pale in comparison to those offered by better prompting methods.'
OpenAI o1 (72.9%) substantially underperforms DeepSeek R1 (80.9%) due to depth-specific failure: o1 answers 'Uncertain' for 90% of depth=7 questions (Appendix H.2, Fig 8).

Futureproofing at very high depths 8-11 (Table 11, Appendix H.3): GPT-4o drops from Easy 77.3% to Very Hard 53.0%; DeepSeek R1 drops from Easy 90.0% to Very Hard 65.0% -- both substantially lower than their scores on LogiQA 2.0 (64.5/87.6) and FOLIO (76.3/86.2), showing JustLogic can be made harder than existing benchmarks on demand.

Dataset complexity (Table 3): JustLogic reading difficulty (Flesch-Kincaid) 20.55 and vocabulary 10557 words across >10 domains, comparable to or exceeding FOLIO (18.75 / 4351, >10 domains) and far above synthetic peers CLUTRR (6.67/1396,1 domain) and ProofWriter (0.96/101,0 domains), while retaining unbounded (1-infinity) reasoning depth and infinite argument structures like the synthetic benchmarks.
failure_modes: Appendix I ('Qualitative Analysis of Failure Modes') identifies 4 major failure modes from chain-of-thought analysis: (1) Logical inconsistency -- models generate arguments relying on premises that contradict earlier steps in their own CoT (example: o1 asserts 'we cannot definitively state R -> S' then later reasoning implicitly assumes R -> S). (2) Wrong application of argument forms -- model identifies the correct argument form (e.g. disjunctive syllogism) but derives the opposite conclusion from it (Llama3-70B example). (3) Using the wrong argument form entirely -- model invokes an argument form (e.g. modus tollens) that cannot logically apply to the premises given, derailing the response into incoherence (Llama3-70B example, reductio ad absurdum should have been used). (4) False interpretation of facts -- mistranslation of a natural-language sentence into the wrong logical form, e.g. GPT-4o failing to recognize that 'some people mistakenly believe that X' entails not-X. Separately, Sec 5.3/Fig 4 finds argument-form-specific weakness: hypothetical syllogism and constructive dilemma have distinctly higher accuracy than modus tollens, disjunctive syllogism, and reductio ad absurdum, hypothesized to correlate with which forms humans use more often. Sec 5.3/Fig 4 also finds accuracy generally decreases monotonically with reasoning depth across all models, and Appendix H.2 documents o1-specific failure: anomalously short responses and premature 'Uncertain' answers (90% of depth=7 items) at high depth, suggestive of constrained test-time compute rather than a reasoning-capability ceiling per se.
one_shot_vs_multistep: The paper's core evaluation is single forward-pass (CQO -> A): the model reads the full premise paragraph plus query once and emits one label, with or without demonstrations/CoT rationale in that same single generation. The paper does test one genuinely multi-step/interactive loop -- tree-of-thought (Appendix E, H.1) -- where the model alternates between a 'generate 3 candidate next steps' prompt and a 'choose the best next step' prompt across up to depth+2 turns, with an explicit halting rule ('models tend to conclude too early; the prompt should be designed to emphasize exploration'). ToT's payoff was inconsistent and mostly negative: it helped only GPT-4o (71.4% vs 65.6% CoT, the single best result in Table 10) while it substantially hurt Llama3-8B (38.6% vs 57.8% CoT) and GPT-4o-mini (48.6% vs 51.8%) and only modestly helped Llama3-70B (60.6% vs 64.6%, still below CoT). The authors conclude 'prompting techniques that are more expensive than vanilla CoT offer little to no performance advantage' and that ToT is 'too complex for most models to utilize, often hallucinating across prompts and failing to break down the problem into coherent steps' (Appendix H.1) -- i.e., for this task family, one well-formed forward pass with worked CoT demonstrations captures most of what smaller/mid models can do, and an explicit multi-turn search loop adds risk (looping, incoherence) more often than it adds accuracy, except for the strongest non-reasoning model tested. Separately, the paper frames reasoning models (o1, DeepSeek R1) as having internalized a multi-step deliberation process via RL training rather than needing an externally imposed interaction loop -- 'reasoning models do not require specific prompting techniques due to their reasoning-specific training' (Appendix H.1) -- and hypothesizes RL-on-CoT training explains DeepSeek R1's and o1's within-single-call advantage over vanilla CoT prompting on non-reasoning models (Sec 5.2).

relation_to_candidate.position: JustLogic sits squarely on the single-shot, single-label-output axis of the c18 family: each instance is a one-shot CQO -> A classification (paragraph + query -> True/False/Uncertain), scored by exact match, identical in shape to c18's proposed 'model emits True/False/Unknown, scored by exact match' design. The one interactive variant tested (tree-of-thought, Appendix E/H.1) is a secondary ablation, not the paper's main protocol, and it manipulates only the elicitation loop, not the observation content per se (the model still only ever sees the same premises+query; ToT just restructures how it accumulates/edits its own reasoning across turns before emitting the final label). The verifier in JustLogic is the generator's own known ground truth (derived deterministically from the synthesized argument structure, Sec 3.1/3.3, Appendix B pseudocode) -- functionally the same 'proof engine as oracle' verifier type c18 specifies, though JustLogic's engine is not described as separately re-deriving/naming a failed inference step for diagnosis (that finer diagnostic granularity is unique to c18's proposal, closer to PrOntoQA's per-step proof tracing).
relation_to_candidate.overlap_with_c18: Extremely high structural overlap: both use programmatically generated theories (facts + rules) at controlled reasoning/argument depth (JustLogic depths 1-7 uniform-split across 7000 instances, Sec 4; c18's D0-D5 bins), both use a closed set of formal argument forms as latent difficulty dials (JustLogic's 7 named forms in Appendix A almost exactly match c18's PrOntoQA/RuleTaker-style rule chaining), both report depth-stratified accuracy curves showing monotonic decline (JustLogic Fig 4/Sec 5.3; matches c18's 'depth bins give both the incremental ladder and a known ceiling'), both explicitly manage contamination/knowledge-leakage risk (JustLogic via randomized truth-values over real GenericsKB sentences plus its dedicated prior-knowledge-independence test, Sec 5.1; c18 via fresh fictional symbols per run), and both are code-generated/regenerable at will to future-proof against saturation and leakage (JustLogic Sec 3.5/Appendix H.3 vs c18's seeded prontoqa generator).
relation_to_candidate.differences: JustLogic uses real-world (but truth-randomized) natural-language sentences drawn from GenericsKB-Best rather than c18's fully fictional/nonce predicates ('every wumpus is a yumpus') -- this is JustLogic's central differentiator (natural-language complexity) and the main axis on which it diverges from the PrOntoQA-style approach c18 inherits; JustLogic explicitly measures and reports this natural-language complexity (Flesch-Kincaid, vocabulary/domain counts, Table 3) as a benchmark property, something c18 does not appear to instrument. JustLogic has 3 answer options (True/False/Uncertain over an open-world assumption) matching c18's own 3-way label space; JustLogic's construction process (Appendix B pseudocode) builds argument trees via level-order combination of 7 named argument forms rather than c18's rule-chaining-plus-distractor-plus-CWA/OWA-toggle design, and JustLogic does not appear to include a constraint-satisfaction/CSP stratum (c18's seating-puzzle addition) or c18's explicit CWA-vs-Unknown pinning gate. JustLogic reports large-scale human baselining (18 MTurk participants, Appendix G) that c18's dossier materials do not describe having replicated. JustLogic's failure-mode analysis (Appendix I) is done qualitatively on free-text CoT traces rather than via a forward-chaining/proof-step oracle; c18's design explicitly plans an independent forward-chaining fixpoint re-derivation of the label (per its implementation notes) specifically because it does not trust the definitional negation-flag label alone -- a soundness safeguard JustLogic's paper does not describe using.
relation_to_candidate.what_c18_would_add: Beyond what JustLogic demonstrates, c18 (per its own candidate spec) would add: (i) fully fictional/nonce ontologies rather than real-world sentences with randomized truth values, trading JustLogic's natural-language complexity for stronger contamination-resistance and simpler surface forms; (ii) an explicit CWA-vs-open-world toggle and negation-as-failure vs explicit-negation contrast as a named latent difficulty dial, rather than JustLogic's single open-world convention; (iii) a second constraint-satisfaction (CSP/seating-puzzle) stratum diversifying beyond pure propositional-logic argument chains; (iv) an independent forward-chaining fixpoint oracle that re-derives the gold label from the theory rather than trusting the generator's definitional negation flag, directly hardening against the generation-soundness risk that JustLogic's paper does not appear to address; and (v) run-verified deterministic seeded regeneration (byte-identical outputs at fixed seed) as an implementation-level guarantee, which JustLogic's paper asserts as a design property (Sec 3.5, 'a new test set of similar difficulty can be trivially generated') but does not document as independently reproducibility-tested the way c18's repo review claims for asaparov/prontoqa.

corpus_claim: JustLogic (Chen, Zhang & Tao, 2025) is a synthetic, prior-knowledge-independent deductive benchmark with fine-grained error analysis over reasoning depth and argument form; a direct 2025 successor to the c18 family. (Acquisition found ICML 2025 acceptance in the LaTeX source; the corpus listed it as just 'arXiv 2025'.)
corpus_claim_verdict: partially-supported. The substantive characterization is fully supported by the paper text: JustLogic is explicitly synthetic/programmatically generated (Sec 3, Abstract), explicitly designed and empirically tested for prior-knowledge independence (Sec 4.1/5.1, Table 5/9), and explicitly performs fine-grained error analysis over reasoning depth and argument form (Sec 5.3, Appendix H/I) -- this is a core, author-stated contribution (Table 1, Sec 1's threefold contribution list). Its status as a 'direct 2025 successor to the c18 family' is reasonable given its literal citation and comparison of PrOntoQA-OOD, ProofWriter, and CLUTRR as direct predecessors (Sec 2.1, Table 1, Appendix C), and its 2025 vintage is correct (v1 2025-01-24). However, the venue detail needs correction: the extraction's own header/discrepancies note states the ICML 2025 acceptance is inferred only from the LaTeX source's use of the icml2025.sty style with the [accepted] option -- it is 'strongly implied' but 'not independently confirmed by arXiv metadata' (no journal-ref on the abs page). So the corrected framing should read: 'venue: arXiv 2025 preprint; LaTeX source formatting (icml2025.sty, accepted option) suggests but does not confirm ICML 2025 acceptance' rather than stating ICML 2025 acceptance as a settled fact.
baseline_relevant: Instance scale/split: 7000 total instances, evenly split across reasoning depths 1-7 (1000 per depth), divided 70%/15%/15% into train/validation/test (Sec 4). Train/validation sets exist specifically to support in-context-learning demo sourcing or fine-tuning.

Difficulty dials demonstrated to matter: reasoning depth (monotonic accuracy decline, Sec 5.3/Fig 4; extended to depths 8-11 in Appendix H.3/Table 11 with clear further degradation) and argument form (heterogeneous accuracy: hypothetical syllogism/constructive dilemma easy, modus tollens/disjunctive syllogism/reductio ad absurdum hard, Sec 5.3/Fig 4).

Prompt format effects on accuracy are large and inconsistent across models: CoT typically beats zero-shot/few-shot for non-reasoning models (e.g., Llama3-70B 53.1->64.6%, Table 6) but few-shot without CoT rationale can underperform zero-shot (Llama3-8B few-shot 41.8% < zero-shot 49.8%, Table 6) -- few-shot demo count itself was tuned (3-shot chosen over 6/9-shot as the highest-accuracy setting, Appendix E), a concrete design choice relevant to un-optimized-baseline prompt selection.
Explicit mention of propositional-logic/argument-form names in the prompt preamble measurably helps: 'CoT w/o propositional logic' underperforms standard CoT for most models (Appendix H.1, Table 10).

More expensive multi-step protocols (self-consistency-CoT, tree-of-thought) mostly do NOT improve on vanilla CoT and can substantially hurt smaller models (Table 10) -- evidence against needing an interactive/multi-turn baseline to establish a reasonable floor.

Ceilings/floors observed: human ceiling 100.0%, human average 73.0% (Table 6, Sec 5.2); best model (DeepSeek R1) 80.9%; random-choice baseline is 33.3% (3-way label) and JustLogic's own no-context-prior-knowledge accuracy is 33.7% (Table 5) essentially at random floor, confirming the task cannot be solved by prior knowledge alone -- a directly relevant floor check for c18's un-optimized baseline design.

Contamination/data-release status: code and full dataset generator publicly released at https://github.com/michaelchen-lab/JustLogic (CC BY 4.0 paper license); the paper explicitly frames the synthetic/regenerable nature as leakage mitigation -- 'Should JustLogic's test set be leaked, a new test set of similar difficulty can be trivially generated' (Sec 3.5) -- meaning any reuse of the exact released test split should be treated as potentially contamination-risked for future models, consistent with c18's own design principle of regenerating fresh instances rather than reusing released sets.

Model-access practicalities: due to limited API access/cost, only 70 random samples were used for OpenAI o1 and 350 for other reasoning models (vs. presumably the full ~1050-item test-depth-stratified set for non-reasoning models), with depth-proportional stratified sampling to preserve fairness (Sec 4.2) -- a relevant precedent for how to size an un-optimized baseline's eval subset under budget constraints.

Known generation caveat overlapping with c18's own risk notes: JustLogic's label correctness depends on correctly tracking factual-accuracy-independent truth values (Sec 3.2, Appendix F.2) -- the paper had to run a dedicated ablation to confirm models weren't failing/succeeding based on real-world factual plausibility of conclusions rather than the stated premises, directly analogous to c18's own concern about CWA-vs-Unknown ambiguity and definitional (non-independently-verified) gold labels.
quotes: "we introduce JustLogic, a synthetically generated deductive reasoning benchmark designed for rigorous evaluation of LLMs... (i) highly complex... (ii) prior knowledge independent... and (iii) capable of in-depth error analysis on the heterogeneous effects of reasoning depth and argument form on model accuracy." (Abstract)

"Most SOTA LLMs performed significantly lower than the average human accuracy (73.0%). Only DeepSeek R1 performed substantially better (80.9%), but still fell short of the human ceiling (100.0%)." (Sec 1, Introduction)
"The JustLogic benchmark's |Delta| is 0.4%, given an accuracy of (33.7%) and random probability (33.3%), which is much lower than other benchmarks, including synthetic ones." (Sec 5.1, Table 5)
"the best-performing model by a large margin is DeepSeek R1 with an accuracy of 80.9%. The second and third-best models, OpenAI o1 and GPT-4o, achieved 72.9% and 65.6%." (Sec 5.2, Table 6)

"the improvements offered by increasing model size pale in comparison to those offered by better prompting methods. Using chain-of-thought prompting, Llama3-8B achieved higher performance (57.8%) than zero-shot Llama3-70B (53.1%)." (Sec 5.2)

"As for reasoning depth, model accuracies generally decrease as depth increases... OpenAI o1-mini performs comparably to DeepSeek R1 at low depths, but o1-mini sees a sharp decline in performance once depth increases, while DeepSeek R1 only sees a moderate decline." (Sec 5.3)

"o1 prematurely answers 'Uncertain' for 90% of questions of depth=7 without faithfully engaging with the question... These observations suggest that OpenAI o1's test-time compute may have been artificially limited." (Appendix H.2)

"we find that prompting techniques that are more expensive than vanilla CoT offer little to no performance advantage... Tree-of-thought is too complex for most models to utilize, often hallucinating across prompts and failing to break down the problem into coherent steps." (Appendix H.1)

corrections: novelty field: 'Flesch-Kincaid 20.55, >10557 vocabulary across >10 domains' corrected to '10557 vocabulary' -- the exact vocabulary count in Table 3 is 10557 (not '>10557'); the '>' belongs only to the '>10 domains' figure and had bled into the adjacent vocabulary number.

protocol.description: corrected the claim that SC-CoT/ToT/CoT-w/o-logic 'were tested... for two models.' Table 10 (which the dossier's own models array and headline_numbers draw on) reports these values for all four non-reasoning models (Llama3-8B, Llama3-70B, GPT-4o-mini, GPT-4o), even though Appendix H.1's prose says the authors selected only 'the best small and large non-reasoning models, Llama3-8B-Instruct and GPT-4o' -- this is an inconsistency present in the source paper itself (prose vs. its own table), and the dossier's description text was flattening it to the prose claim while the rest of the dossier used the table's 4-model data. Fixed to note both the table coverage and the paper's internal prose/table discrepancy.
_verification.note: Systematically re-checked every number in Tables 3, 5, 6, 9, 10, and 11 against the source markdown transcription (all matched, no column-misalignment errors found); verified all 8 verbatim quotes against source prose (all matched, ellipses used only for legitimate elision); verified protocol_mode classification (single-shot with tree-of-thought correctly carved out as the one genuinely multi-turn/interactive ablation, tested on a subset of models only, not the paper's main protocol) and confirmed self-consistency-CoT is multi-sample-single-turn, not multi-turn, so it was correctly not classified as interactive; verified observation_content's paraphrased general-instruction prompt and 7-argument-form list against Appendix E verbatim text (matched); verified all four Appendix I failure-mode examples' model attributions (o1, Llama3-70B x2, GPT-4o) against source verbatim excerpts (all correctly attributed); verified sample sizes (70 for o1, 350 for other reasoning models), human participant count (18), and futureproofing Table 11 figures (all matched). Found two minor transcription/internal-consistency issues (both corrected above); no errors found in any headline accuracy figure, model name, or protocol classification.
